answer:
**In *Traité de la propriété* Vol. I, Chapter 7, Charles Comte establishes that all habitable lands on Earth have long been occupied by families grouped into hordes, tribes, or nations—including "savage" or indigenous peoples—and that these groups treat delimited territories as their exclusive national property, even if individuals within them do not cultivate land intensively.**[^1][^2] He illustrates this with travelers' accounts from regions like Louisiana, North America, Arabia, and Tierra del Fuego: savage hordes defend hunting grounds as "national property" with fierce wars, selling land only when compensated (as Anglo-Americans did peacefully in Pennsylvania, unlike usurpers), but lack individual territorial property since they prioritize mobility over fixed cultivation.[^3]

**Vol. II, in Chapter 47, introduces a key theoretical nuance by critiquing jurists (e.g., Toullier) who—echoing Rousseau, Grotius, and others—posit a "primitive" or "natural" state of "negative community" where land belonged to no one (unowned/res nullius), with only fruits or moveables going to the "first occupant."**[^4] Comte rejects this as fictional, arguing it ignores empirical realities like those detailed in Vol. I: men have always formed families and tribes claiming territories as property from time immemorial, with no historical evidence of unoccupied lands. This "negative community" fantasy, per Comte, wrongly separates property from natural human associations and labor, conflating it with mere civil-law guarantees rather than inherent occupation by groups (including primitives).

**Vol. I thus empirically "proved" continuous tribal/indigenous occupation as foundational property (contra abstract vacancy), but "missed" explicitly dismantling the juristic theories positing pre-social unowned land—a theoretical "wrinkle" Vol. II supplies by tying Vol. I's facts to a broader critique of flawed origins narratives (e.g., Rousseau's isolated individuals, Grotius's post-Deluge commons).** This resolves why primitives defend land as property despite minimal individual appropriation: tribal/national claims predate "civil" states.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Ch. 7 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-523da2).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Ch. 7 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-bb287d).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Ch. 7 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/07-du-territoire-propre-a-chaque-nation/translation/#p-9af6f6).

[^4]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL II*, Ch. 47 [paragraph](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-ii-6beea5/47-des-opinions-des-jurisconsultes-sur-lorigine-et-la/translation/#p-7bd3ed).
